metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add to the 'What' section an explicit description of how session-ID replay is prevented (SDK guarantee with cited evidence, or a custom middleware step), or (b) add an AC that verifies the SDK's session-isolation behavior as a documented property — so AC5 has corresponding described work.
evidence: AC5: 'an Mcp-Session-Id obtained by one authenticated client, replayed by a DIFFERENT client that presents no valid token of its own, is NEVER served in the first principal's authenticated context'
'What' wiring paragraph: 'when auth_enabled, pass token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…) and auth=AuthSettings(…) to FastMCP' — no mention of session-ID isolation logic
Approach section: 'Model the framework on the SDK's own TokenVerifier/AuthSettings seam' — delegates to SDK without verifying SDK provides session replay protection
ABSENCE: no plan text describes implementing or testing (via SDK inspection/docs) that session-ID replay is blocked by the SDK's BearerAuthBackend
findings.finding: AC5 (session-id replay) tests a security property — that a session-ID obtained by client A cannot be replayed by client B presenting no valid token — but the 'What' section describes no implementation of session-ID isolation logic, and the plan makes no assertion that the FastMCP SDK provides this guarantee. The AC therefore measures a behavior with no described implementation, making it either an uncovered deliverable or an untested assumption about SDK internals.
findings.location: AC5 ('Integration test asserts session-id replay cannot hijack a principal...') vs. 'What' section (build_server wiring paragraph)
scenarios: The FastMCP SDK's session handling may allow session-ID reuse across unauthenticated requests — if so AC5 would pass in test but the property wouldn't hold in production
An implementer has no described logic to write for this AC and may either skip it or make incorrect assumptions about SDK behavior
If the SDK does not isolate sessions by bearer token, a real attacker could replay a session-ID from an authenticated exchange in an unauthenticated request
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add a sentence such as: 'Verify (via SDK changelog/source for mcp 1.28.1) that `BearerAuthBackend` binds session context to the authenticated principal such that a bare `Mcp-Session-Id` header without a valid token is rejected; document the SDK version + behavior as the basis for AC5.' If no such guarantee exists, describe a thin middleware shim that rejects requests carrying a known session-ID but no valid bearer token.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan step that either (a) cites the specific SDK behavior guaranteeing per-request token re-verification regardless of `Mcp-Session-Id`, OR (b) describes an additional middleware/hook that enforces per-request auth re-evaluation when the SDK does not provide this natively.
evidence: AC5: 'an Mcp-Session-Id obtained by one authenticated client, replayed by a DIFFERENT client that presents no valid token of its own, is NEVER served in the first principal's authenticated context'

What/build_server wiring: 'when auth_enabled, pass token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…) and auth=AuthSettings(…) to FastMCP' — no step addresses session-ID binding or per-request auth re-evaluation
Approach & rationale: 'Model the framework on the SDK's own TokenVerifier/AuthSettings seam' — delegates to SDK without verifying or stating the SDK's per-request vs per-session auth behavior
ABSENCE: no plan text inspects, asserts, or documents whether FastMCP's Streamable-HTTP handler calls the TokenVerifier on every request or only on session establishment
findings.finding: AC5 (session-ID replay cannot hijack a principal) names a security goal with no described implementation mechanism in the plan body. The plan wires `token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(...)` and `auth=AuthSettings(...)` into FastMCP and says nothing about whether the SDK re-authenticates on every request or caches auth state by `Mcp-Session-Id`. If the SDK's Streamable-HTTP handler binds auth state to a session-ID (a plausible optimization), the described wiring alone does not achieve AC5's guarantee — additional per-request middleware or explicit SDK verification would be required. Without stating which SDK mechanism provides this guarantee (or adding a middleware step when it does not), the plan describes an unverifiable or vacuously-passing test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5; 'What' section — 'Wire it in build_server' paragraph; 'Approach & rationale' section
scenarios: FastMCP's Streamable-HTTP handler authenticates once per session and caches the result against the session-ID header; a replayed session-ID with no token succeeds because the composite is never invoked — AC5 fails in production but the test may pass vacuously if the test's session-ID is freshly generated and thus has no cached auth state.

An implementer reads the plan, sees 'pass token_verifier to FastMCP', writes the AC5 test, and gets a green result because the session-ID in the test was never authenticated — giving false assurance that the replay property is enforced.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Wire it in build_server' paragraph, add: 'Verify (by inspecting FastMCP's Streamable-HTTP ASGI handler) whether the SDK invokes `token_verifier` on every request or only on session establishment. If the SDK caches auth by session-ID, add a thin per-request middleware ahead of FastMCP that re-evaluates the bearer token on every request and denies if absent/invalid, so the session-ID replay property (AC5) is enforced mechanically rather than by untested SDK assumption.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a mechanism statement in the What or Security section explaining how per-request bearer-token re-validation is enforced (e.g., 'the SDK's BearerAuthBackend validates the Authorization header on every request independently of session state'), or note explicitly that this is SDK-guaranteed (with a version-pinned citation) and that AC5 tests it as an oracle against the running ASGI stack.
evidence: AC5 says: 'an Mcp-Session-Id obtained by one authenticated client, replayed by a DIFFERENT client that presents no valid token of its own, is NEVER served in the first principal's authenticated context'
ABSENCE: the What / Approach / Security sections contain no mechanism description for how per-request token re-validation is enforced at the HTTP layer
Plan says: 'pass token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…) and auth=AuthSettings(…) to FastMCP' — defers session handling entirely to the SDK with no further specification
findings.finding: AC5 asserts a session-ID replay security property (a session-ID from client A cannot be used by client B with no valid token) but the plan body — What, Approach, Security — contains no described implementation mechanism. The plan delegates to FastMCP + AuthSettings without specifying whether the SDK re-validates the bearer token on every request or caches authentication state per session-ID. If the SDK caches auth state by session-ID (a common pattern), an unauthenticated actor replaying a leaked session-ID could access the first principal's context without presenting any token.
findings.location: AC5; What → Wire it in `build_server`; Security section
scenarios: An attacker observes a valid session-ID from a legitimate client (e.g., via a shared log or HTTP header leak) and replays it without a bearer token, gaining access if the SDK's session middleware trusts the session-ID alone.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the startup guard rules (and add to AC7) what happens when `auth_enabled=true` is combined with `transport=stdio`: either refuse to start with a clear error (recommended, consistent with fail-closed) or emit a loud warning that auth is non-operational on stdio.
evidence: 'auth_enabled with transport=http where no verifier is wired refuses to start (never a silent open endpoint)'

AC7: 'Unit test asserts fail-closed startup: an unknown auth_strategies entry, an empty composite with auth_enabled=true, and auth_enabled + transport=http with no verifier wired each refuse to start'
ABSENCE: no mention of auth_enabled=true + transport=stdio behavior in either the startup guard enumeration or AC7's test cases
Compatibility note says 'auth off by default' and scope is HTTP transport, but does not state what happens when auth_enabled=true is set on a stdio-only deployment
findings.finding: The startup guard logic covers `auth_enabled=true` + `transport=http` with no verifier (refuse to start), but leaves the `auth_enabled=true` + `transport=stdio` combination unspecified. Since stdio MCP does not use HTTP bearer authentication, silently enabling auth_enabled on a stdio server either (a) silently no-ops (a dangerous misconfiguration footgun — operator believes auth is on, it is not), or (b) should refuse to start. The plan's fail-closed philosophy implies (b), but no AC or implementation step addresses this edge.
findings.location: What section — factory/startup error rules; Security section — 'refuse start' enumeration; AC7
scenarios: An operator sets auth_enabled=true in rebar.toml while running the stdio transport (e.g., via Claude Desktop). The server starts silently, ignores auth, and the operator believes token auth is enforced when it is not.
A deployment rotates from stdio to HTTP and expects auth_enabled=true to be already guarded — but the stdio-era config silently passed, giving a false sense that the config was validated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the factory startup-error enumeration: '`auth_enabled=true` with `transport=stdio` is a hard startup error (auth has no effect on stdio; the operator must explicitly set `auth_enabled=false` to run unauthenticated stdio).' Add a corresponding case to AC7.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan note (in Approach or AC5) citing whether the SDK's BearerAuthBackend re-authenticates on every request (stateless) or caches by session-id, and — if caching — name the additional middleware/step S2 adds to enforce per-request principal binding; the AC oracle must then map to that step.
evidence: AC5: 'an Mcp-Session-Id obtained by one authenticated client, replayed by a DIFFERENT client that presents no valid token of its own, is NEVER served in the first principal's authenticated context'
Plan body describes only: 'pass token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…) and auth=AuthSettings(…) to FastMCP' — no step addresses session-id binding or per-request auth re-evaluation
ABSENCE: no mention of SDK session-id vs auth caching behavior, no custom middleware step for session isolation
findings.finding: AC5 (session-id replay cannot hijack a principal) has no described implementation step in the plan body. The plan wires `token_verifier` and `AuthSettings` into FastMCP but says nothing about per-request re-authentication vs session-id caching. If the SDK's Streamable-HTTP handler re-checks the bearer token on every request the AC is satisfied by SDK behavior alone; if it caches auth by session-id the AC would require additional middleware — and the plan contains no step for either case. An executor cannot know which path to implement, and a green test could pass vacuously if the session-id header is simply ignored.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (session-id replay); Approach & rationale / Wire it in build_server section
scenarios: Executor implements the wiring step, writes the session-replay test, and the test passes vacuously because the SDK ignores Mcp-Session-Id entirely — giving false confidence without actually enforcing the claimed property.
SDK caches auth by session-id; replayed session + no token succeeds; no step in the plan addresses this; the AC is unreachable by the described steps.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'The SDK's BearerAuthBackend evaluates the Authorization header on every request independently of Mcp-Session-Id (stateless); no session-id cache exists, so replay without a valid bearer token receives 401 automatically — confirmed against mcp 1.28.1. AC5 tests this property.' If that statement is NOT true, add a step: 'Register a middleware in build_server that binds session-id → authenticated principal and rejects any request whose session-id does not match its own bearer token.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the expires_at ambiguity for StaticBearerVerifier: explicitly state what value is assigned (e.g., 'omit expires_at / set to None to signal no expiry, consistent with SDK behavior') and reconcile with the 'never 0' rule in the Security section.
evidence: Security section: 'a positive expires_at is set where a token has one (never 0, which the SDK treats as no-expiry)'
What section: 'Static tokens are dev/internal-grade, ≥128-bit, and non-expiring (documented in S7)'
The plan never says whether StaticBearerVerifier sets expires_at=None, omits it, or uses 0 — and forbids the only SDK-documented no-expiry sentinel (0)
findings.finding: The plan states 'a positive expires_at is set where a token has one (never 0, which the SDK treats as no-expiry)' in the Security section, yet static tokens are declared 'non-expiring' — the plan never resolves what value StaticBearerVerifier assigns to expires_at on the returned AccessToken. Setting 0 would match the 'no-expiry' SDK sentinel but is explicitly forbidden; omitting the field or using None may behave differently from 0 in the SDK. The counter/field assignment is ambiguously placed with no authoritative resolution.
findings.location: Security section — 'a positive expires_at is set where a token has one (never 0, which the SDK treats as no-expiry)'; What section — 'Static tokens … non-expiring'
scenarios: Implementer omits expires_at → SDK may treat missing field differently from 0, possibly causing unexpected token rejection or acceptance
Implementer sets expires_at=0 → violates the 'never 0' rule stated in Security section even though 0 means 'no expiry'
Implementer sets expires_at=None → depends on SDK handling of None, which is unspecified in the plan

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the TLS / HTTPS posture for `auth_enabled=true` + `transport=http`: either (a) add a startup check/warning when the configured `auth_resource_server_url` is an `http://` scheme rather than `https://` (advisory), or (b) document in the Security section that the HTTP transport MUST be TLS-terminated by a reverse proxy and add this to the operator runbook.
evidence: Plan says: 'auth_enabled + transport=http where no verifier is wired refuses to start' — guards only verifier presence, not transport encryption
Plan says: 'pass token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…) … to FastMCP' with no mention of TLS requirement or HTTPS-only enforcement
Bearer tokens traverse the wire in Authorization headers; plaintext HTTP exposes them to passive eavesdroppers
ABSENCE: no mention of TLS, HTTPS, reverse-proxy requirement, or advisory warning in the Security section or Compatibility section
findings.finding: The plan introduces bearer-token-authenticated HTTP MCP endpoints (`transport=http` + `auth_enabled=true`) but never declares a TLS requirement or termination expectation. Bearer tokens in `Authorization` headers sent over plaintext HTTP are fully exposed to network eavesdroppers, making credential interception trivial. The plan's fail-closed guard only checks that a verifier is wired — it says nothing about whether the transport itself must be TLS-encrypted or reverse-proxy-terminated.
findings.location: What → Wire it in `build_server`; Security section; AC2/AC4
scenarios: An operator enables `auth_enabled=true` with `transport=http` behind a non-TLS load balancer — tokens are transmitted in cleartext and can be captured by a network-adjacent attacker.
A developer tests locally on plain HTTP and later promotes the same config to a production environment, unaware that TLS is not enforced.
findings.suggested_fix: In `build_server`, when `auth_enabled=true` and `transport=http`, check whether `auth_resource_server_url` starts with `https://`; if not, log a `WARNING` (not a hard error, to allow loopback/test scenarios) and document this check in the Security section. Add a note to the compatibility/security docs that production deployment requires TLS termination in front of the MCP HTTP endpoint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit/integration AC asserting that `StaticBearerVerifier` (or `build_server`) produces a hard startup error with a clear message for each of: (a) `auth_static_tokens_file` not found or unreadable, (b) `auth_static_tokens_file` is not valid JSON/TOML, and (c) a `token_env` entry names an env var that is absent/empty at startup time.
evidence: AC8 covers: SHA-256-only storage, constant-time compare, resource stamping, rejection of 'plaintext literal or missing both fields' — but no AC covers file-not-found, file-parse-error, or token_env→absent-env-var
Plan says verifier 'resolves plaintext from token_env and stores only its SHA-256' but specifies no test for the case where that env var does not exist at load time

'a record embedding a plaintext literal, or missing both fields, is rejected at startup' — partial coverage; structural errors in the file itself (missing file, malformed JSON/TOML) are unaddressed by any AC
findings.finding: The testing plan has no AC covering startup failure paths for `StaticBearerVerifier` when `auth_static_tokens_file` is missing/unreadable/unparseable, and when a `token_env` entry names an env var that is absent or empty at load time. For a security-critical component whose entire secrets-supply chain depends on these paths being fatal errors, the absence of explicit test oracles means a misconfig could silently produce a server that starts but rejects all tokens (or, depending on implementation, has a weaker initialization state) with no clear diagnostic — and CI would not catch a regressed startup guard.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC8 / `StaticBearerVerifier` startup rejection block; Static-token secrets format section ("token_env" description)
scenarios: Operator sets auth_static_tokens_file to a path that doesn't exist yet in a container — server should refuse to start but no AC mandates this behavior is tested
token_env names MCP_SECRET but that var is not set in the deployment env — server could silently start with zero active tokens, causing all auth to fail with no clear startup error
Malformed JSON in the secrets file (e.g., a TOML file named with .json extension) — parse error should be a hard startup failure with a clear message, but no AC verifies this
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC8 (or add AC8b) with: 'Unit test asserts StaticBearerVerifier raises a clear startup error when auth_static_tokens_file is missing, when the file is not parseable as JSON/TOML, and when a record's token_env names an env var that is unset or empty at load time (observable oracle: raised exception carries a message identifying the bad record/file path — no token or secret in the message).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in the What section and AC8) that `StaticBearerVerifier` must reject at startup any `token_env` entry whose resolved plaintext is an empty string, treating it as a misconfiguration equivalent to a missing secret.
evidence: Plan says: 'The verifier resolves plaintext from `token_env` and stores only its SHA-256'
Plan says: 'a record embedding a plaintext literal, or missing both fields, is rejected at startup' — empty-string env var is neither case
ABSENCE: no specification that an empty-string resolution from `token_env` must be rejected as a startup error
sha256('') = 'e3b0c44298fc1c149afb…' is a known constant; any attacker can compute it
findings.finding: The `token_env` secret-resolution path does not specify that an env var resolving to an empty string is a startup error. If `token_env` names a variable that is set-but-empty (e.g. an accidentally blanked secret in a deployment env), the verifier would silently store `sha256('')` as the expected digest. Any client presenting `Authorization: Bearer <hex-of-sha256-of-empty-string>` would then be accepted — a trivially computable credential that bypasses authentication entirely.
findings.location: What → Static-token secrets format; What → StaticBearerVerifier; AC8
scenarios: An operator sets `token_env=MY_TOKEN` in the secrets file but forgets to populate `MY_TOKEN` in the environment. The server starts silently, accepts `Authorization: Bearer e3b0c44298fc1c149afb...` from any client.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the StaticBearerVerifier load step: 'after resolving plaintext from `token_env`, if the result is an empty string, raise a hard startup error (same class as missing-both-fields).' Add a corresponding AC8 assertion: 'a `token_env` entry whose env var is set-but-empty is rejected at startup.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the `auth_strategies` `_SECTIONS['mcp']` coercer validates individual entries against the closed set `{static, jwt, introspection, proxy, custom}` or merely tokenizes (and if the latter, explicitly state that the factory re-validates each item) — and either document the return type of the coercer or name a new helper to be introduced.
evidence: Plan: 'auth_strategies (comma-separated → ordered list)' — this matches _as_str_tuple semantics (tokenize, no per-value validation)
Plan: 'value vocabulary is the CLOSED set static | jwt | introspection | proxy | custom. An unrecognized entry is a hard startup error' — validation is deferred to the factory
Codebase: _as_str_tuple (src/rebar/_config_schema.py:99) does not validate individual values; _as_choice (line 115) validates a single value; no list-of-choices coercer variant exists
The plan never specifies the return type of the auth_strategies coercer or whether the factory consumes a pre-validated or unvalidated sequence
findings.finding: The plan splits auth_strategies validation across two sites without specifying their interface: the flat _SECTIONS['mcp'] coercer entry is described as 'comma-separated → ordered list' (tokenize only, no per-value validation), while the factory enforces the closed enum {'static','jwt','introspection','proxy','custom'} as a 'hard startup error' — but the plan never says whether the coercer's return type is a raw tuple of unvalidated strings (requiring the factory to re-validate each entry) or whether a list-of-choices coercer variant should be introduced. An implementer cannot determine the coercer signature without resolving this split.
findings.location: What section — 'auth_strategies (comma-separated → ordered list)' + factory description 'An unrecognized entry is a hard startup error'
scenarios: Implementer adds a raw _as_str_tuple coercer → factory must iterate and re-validate each entry against the closed set (undocumented contract)
Implementer adds a per-element validation coercer → must introduce a new helper not described in the plan
Both sites validate inconsistently → startup error messages differ between coerce_sparse and factory phases, confusing operators
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The `auth_strategies` coercer entry uses `_as_str_tuple` (tokenize only, no per-value enum check); the factory iterates the returned tuple and raises `ConfigError` on any unrecognized entry so that the startup error is issued at factory construction time with the full closed-set vocabulary in the message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a specified fallback for the case where `token_env` names an env var that is not set at startup: declare whether this is a hard startup error (preferred, fail-closed) or a logged warning + skip, and add an AC or unit-test assertion covering this path.
evidence: Plan: 'The verifier resolves plaintext from token_env and stores only its SHA-256; a record embedding a plaintext literal, or missing both fields, is rejected at startup.'
Plan does not mention what happens when token_env is present but os.environ[token_env] raises KeyError
AC8 tests 'rejects a secrets-file record with a plaintext literal or missing both token_env/token_sha256' but no AC covers the unset-env-var case
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the fallback behavior when a `token_env`-sourced secret record references an env var that is not set in the environment at startup time. The plan covers two rejection cases ('plaintext literal embedded' and 'missing both token_env and token_sha256') but is silent on the case where `token_env` is present but the named variable is absent from `os.environ`.
findings.location: What section — 'Static-token secrets format' paragraph; StaticBearerVerifier description
scenarios: Operator sets token_env=MY_PAT but forgets to export MY_PAT → server either starts silently (token entry ignored, effective auth weakened) or hard-errors with an unclear message
Different implementations guess different behaviors: one raises ConfigError, another skips with a warning, another raises KeyError with no context
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the StaticBearerVerifier description: 'If token_env is present but the named variable is absent from os.environ, the verifier raises ConfigError at startup (fail-closed — a missing secret is never silently skipped).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_*` env vars (auto-derived through `_SECTIONS['mcp']`, not direct `os.environ` reads) will appear in the `gen_env_registry.py`-generated `docs/env-vars.md` — either extend `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` / add a config-derived allowlist in the generator, or explicitly add entries, so the CI drift gate passes.
evidence: scripts/gen_env_registry.py scans for KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS and direct `os.environ["X"]` / `os.environ.get("X")` / `os.getenv("X")` literals only
New auth config keys are resolved via config.py's env_overrides() loop over _SECTIONS — no direct os.environ literal reads in _mcp_auth.py for the config layer

Plan says 'register in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json and regenerate docs/mcp-reference.md/docs/env-vars.md' without explaining how the auto-derived env vars will surface in the scanner-generated env-vars.md
docs/env-vars.md line 1: 'Generated by scripts/gen_env_registry.py — do not edit by hand'
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` is an unenumerated consumer whose output (`docs/env-vars.md`) will NOT automatically include the new `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_*` env vars. The new auth keys are consumed through `env_overrides()` → `_SECTIONS['mcp']` (auto-derived `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_ENABLED` etc.), not via direct `os.environ["X"]` / `os.environ.get("X")` literal reads that the generator scans for. The plan states 'regenerate docs/env-vars.md' but does not address this scanner gap, meaning the CI drift gate for `docs/env-vars.md` will fail or silently omit the new auth env vars.
findings.location: What section ('register in MCP_ENV_VARS/server.json and regenerate docs/mcp-reference.md/docs/env-vars.md') and AC1
scenarios: CI runs `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py --check` after S2 lands; the new REBAR_MCP_AUTH_ENABLED etc. env vars are absent from the generated output because the scanner can't see them through the config abstraction; the drift gate either fails (blocking CI) or passes with the vars undocumented.
An operator tries to configure auth via env vars and finds no documentation entry in docs/env-vars.md for REBAR_MCP_AUTH_ENABLED.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a config-key-derived emit path to `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` that reads `_SECTIONS['mcp']` and emits the auto-derived `REBAR_MCP_<KEY>` names alongside the scanner output, or add a static allowlist section in the generator for config-layer-only vars.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that a missing, unreadable, or unparseable `auth_static_tokens_file` is a **hard startup error** (not a silent skip), and add a corresponding AC or unit-test assertion covering file-not-found and permission-denied cases.
evidence: Plan says 'a record embedding a plaintext literal, or missing both fields, is rejected at startup' (record-level rejection) but never addresses file-not-found or permission-denied at the file level.
AC8 unit-tests only cover record-level rejections, not file I/O failures.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens if `auth_static_tokens_file` names a path that does not exist or is unreadable at startup — hard startup error vs. silent skip is unspecified. A silent skip would leave auth unexpectedly open if the file path is misconfigured.
findings.location: What section — StaticBearerVerifier / auth_static_tokens_file; Acceptance Criteria AC8
scenarios: Operator typos the file path; file is not mounted in a container; file permissions are wrong — verifier silently skips, potentially leaving auth disabled or raising an opaque error rather than a clear startup failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the StaticBearerVerifier spec: 'If the file named by `auth_static_tokens_file` does not exist, cannot be opened, or is not valid JSON/TOML, the verifier raises a fatal ConfigurationError at startup.' Add an AC8 sub-case: 'A missing or unreadable secrets file refuses to start with a clear message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `_mcp_auth.py`'s redaction helper will reuse or factor out `rebar.llm.failure._redact` (which already covers Bearer tokens, hex digests, and base64) rather than introducing a third independent redactor for the same shapes.
evidence: Plan text: 'No-log redaction: add a redaction helper used on every auth error/log path (composite raise handler, verifier errors) so no token/secret substring is ever emitted.'

src/rebar/llm/failure.py:98–112: `_REDACTIONS` tuple includes `r'(?i)\bbearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-]{8,}'` → `'Bearer [REDACTED]'` and `r'\b[A-Fa-f0-9]{32,}\b'` → `'[REDACTED_HEX]'` and `r'\b[A-Za-z0-9+/]{40,}={0,2}\b'` → `'[REDACTED_B64]'` — covering Bearer tokens, SHA-256 hex digests, and base64-encoded secrets.
src/rebar/llm/workflow/lint.py:51–55: second private `_redact` helper with different partial-masking semantics (already a divergence).
ABSENCE: plan does not reference `rebar.llm.failure._redact` or propose factoring a shared helper.
findings.finding: The plan proposes 'add a redaction helper used on every auth error/log path' in `_mcp_auth.py` without acknowledging that `rebar.llm.failure._redact` already implements a production-grade regex-based redactor covering exactly the relevant shapes (Bearer tokens: `\bbearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-]{8,}`, hex digests: `\b[A-Fa-f0-9]{32,}\b`, and base64: `\b[A-Za-z0-9+/]{40,}={0,2}\b`). Introducing a third private redactor (alongside `llm.failure._redact` and `llm.workflow.lint._redact`) NIHs existing functionality for the same token shapes.
findings.location: What section — 'No-log redaction: add a redaction helper used on every auth error/log path'
scenarios: The new `_mcp_auth.py` redactor diverges from `llm.failure._redact` over time (e.g., a new token shape is added to one but not the other), leaving a gap in one path.
A code reviewer must audit three independent redaction implementations instead of one, increasing the surface for a missed redaction pattern.
findings.suggested_fix: Factor `rebar.llm.failure._redact` (or its `_REDACTIONS` pattern table) into a shared utility (e.g., `rebar._redact` or `rebar._logging`) that both `_mcp_auth.py` and `llm.failure` import, so there is one canonical token-redaction implementation. Alternatively, explicitly justify why a separate, simpler helper is preferred (e.g., if the auth path must stay stdlib-only and `llm.failure` grows non-stdlib deps).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify BearerAuthBackend's `.resource` non-inspection claim against the actual installed SDK source (grep the installed site-packages), and either pin `mcp` to the grounded version or document the version range over which the behavioral claim holds.
evidence: resolve_symbol('BearerAuthBackend', 'mcp.server.auth.middleware.bearer') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('BearerAuthBackend', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
pyproject.toml:82: mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"] — no upper-bound pin, no lock file committed
No uv.lock or poetry.lock found in repo; dependabot.yml explicitly notes 'uv.lock is ever committed'
Security section repeats the behavioral claim: 'a positive expires_at is set where a token has one (never 0, which the SDK treats as no-expiry)' — also unverifiable
findings.finding: The plan's security-critical behavioral claim — 'the SDK's BearerAuthBackend never inspects .resource (grounded against mcp 1.28.1)' — cannot be verified: BearerAuthBackend is not resolvable in the installed environment from any tried module path, no lock file pins the SDK to 1.28.1, and the pyproject.toml constraint is merely mcp>=1.9 (open-ended). If a future mcp version adds or changes audience enforcement in BearerAuthBackend, the composite re-check's rationale ('there is no backstop behind the verifier') may be inaccurate — or conversely, could conflict with new SDK behavior.
findings.location: Approach & rationale: 'the SDK's BearerAuthBackend never inspects .resource (grounded against mcp 1.28.1)'
scenarios: A future mcp upgrade adds resource/audience inspection in BearerAuthBackend, making the composite double-check redundant but harmless — however the plan's rationale that the composite IS the ONLY backstop would be wrong.
More critically: if BearerAuthBackend at an installed version already validates audience differently, the composite logic could interact unexpectedly with SDK internals.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an upper-bound pin (e.g., `mcp>=1.28,<2`) in pyproject.toml's mcp extra, or add a startup assertion / unit test that probes whether the SDK's BearerAuthBackend inspects `resource` and fails clearly if a new version changes the contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm or define the exact name, type, and value vocabulary of the S1-delivered 'transport' config field that S2's startup guard reads, and add it as an explicit stated precondition (not just 'Depends on S1's transport + SDK pin').
evidence: McpConfig at _config_schema.py:388-392 has fields: readonly, allow_llm, allow_jira_sync — no 'transport' field
_SECTIONS['mcp'] at _config_schema.py:538-542 has readonly/allow_llm/allow_jira_sync coercers — no 'transport' entry
search_files for 'allow_unauthenticated_http' → no matches anywhere in src/
search_files for 'transport=http' → no matches in src/
The plan says 'Resolve S1's unauthenticated-HTTP guard against the real auth_enabled'
findings.finding: The plan's AC1 and startup-error AC7 reference 'auth_enabled + transport=http with no verifier wired refuses to start', asserting that S1 delivers a 'transport' config field on McpConfig. Currently McpConfig has only readonly/allow_llm/allow_jira_sync (verified). The plan states 'Depends on S1's transport + SDK pin', but the specific field name and behavior of the transport gate are unverified premises that S2's startup guard depends on.
findings.location: AC7: 'auth_enabled + transport=http with no verifier wired each refuse to start'; Scope: 'Depends on S1's transport + SDK pin'
scenarios: S1 ships a transport field with a different name or different value vocabulary (e.g., 'http' vs 'streamable_http'), causing S2's startup guard to never fire.
S1 is descoped or delayed, leaving S2's transport-based guard with no field to read — the startup check silently doesn't work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the 'BearerAuthBackend never inspects .resource' assertion with a specific code citation (file path + line in the installed mcp SDK) and either pin `mcp>=1.28` in pyproject.toml or add a startup assertion / test that verifies this SDK behavior holds in the installed version.
evidence: 'the SDK's BearerAuthBackend never inspects `.resource` (grounded against mcp 1.28.1) — there is no backstop behind the verifier'
resolve_symbol('BearerAuthBackend', 'mcp.server.auth.middleware.bearer') → UNRESOLVED in the installed environment
resolve_symbol('BearerAuthBackend', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED in the installed environment
pyproject.toml line 82: `mcp = ["mcp>=1.9"]` — open floor spanning 19+ minor versions
ABSENCE: no code citation (file + line number) for the BearerAuthBackend inspection behavior; no test that would fail if the SDK starts checking .resource; no SDK version pin to 1.28.x
findings.finding: The plan's entire composite-audience re-check security model rests on the assertion that `BearerAuthBackend` in the installed mcp SDK 'never inspects `.resource`' (grounded against mcp 1.28.1), but no code citation, test guard, or SDK version pin locks this assertion in place. The symbol `BearerAuthBackend` does not resolve in the installed environment's public API surface, and the plan itself acknowledges this is the 'no backstop' property that makes the composite mandatory — yet the `mcp>=1.9` floor allows any version from 1.9 onward, across which this internal behavior may differ.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section ('the SDK's BearerAuthBackend never inspects `.resource` (grounded against mcp 1.28.1) — there is no backstop behind the verifier'); pyproject.toml line 82 (`mcp>=1.9`)
scenarios: A deployment installs mcp 1.29+ where the SDK has been updated to inspect .resource in BearerAuthBackend — the composite's re-check is now redundant but the plan author had no mechanism to know the SDK backstop was added, and the composite may be incorrectly removed in a cleanup.
A deployment installs mcp 1.9–1.27 where BearerAuthBackend behavior differs from 1.28.1 — the security invariant was never verified for these versions.

A future contributor reviews the composite and, seeing no code citation for why the SDK doesn't backstop .resource, removes the audience re-check as 'redundant' — silently opening the audience bypass.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test in the `_mcp_auth` unit suite that imports `BearerAuthBackend` (or its successor), inspects its `verify`/`authenticate` method, and asserts it does NOT call `token.resource` — this test will fail on a future SDK upgrade that adds the check, making the change visible. Additionally, tighten the floor to `mcp>=1.28` since that is the only version the plan has grounded against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact `FastMCP` constructor parameters for resource-server mode (specifically that `token_verifier=` and `auth=AuthSettings(...)` are the correct kwargs) by citing a concrete SDK example, the SDK source, or a passing spike test, before committing to this wiring design.
evidence: resolve_symbol('token_verifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
The plan conflates the 'simple-auth' example (which may use a full OAuth provider, not a resource-server-only verifier) with the resource-server wiring path
ABSENCE: no concrete FastMCP constructor signature or kwarg list is cited; no example code is included to confirm `FastMCP(token_verifier=..., auth=...)` is the actual API
findings.finding: The plan asserts `FastMCP` accepts `token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(...)` as a constructor argument, but this specific API surface is unverified. `token_verifier` does not resolve as a symbol on `mcp.server.fastmcp`, and the SDK's public documentation of FastMCP constructor parameters for resource-server wiring (vs. the full OAuth authorization-server flow) may not include this keyword — the plan may be confusing the resource-server-mode API with the full OAuth provider API.
findings.location: What section ('pass `token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…)` and `auth=AuthSettings(…)` to `FastMCP`'); Approach & rationale ('Model the framework on the SDK's own `TokenVerifier`/`AuthSettings` seam (the blessed extension point the `simple-auth` example uses)')
scenarios: The FastMCP resource-server mode requires a different constructor pattern (e.g., a separate `with_auth()` builder, or an `oauth_provider=` kwarg) — the plan's wiring code fails at import/runtime with a TypeError.
The `token_verifier` parameter exists but is named differently in the installed SDK version (e.g., `bearer_verifier`, `auth_provider`), requiring a nontrivial rework of the build_server wiring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm by reading `mcp/server/fastmcp/server.py` and `mcp/server/auth/provider.py` source in the installed venv that: (a) `FastMCP.__init__` accepts `token_verifier` and `auth` kwargs; (b) `AuthSettings` has fields named `issuer_url`, `resource_server_url`, and `required_scopes`; (c) `AccessToken` has a field named `resource` — quote the actual field names in the plan to lock the integration surface.
evidence: AuthSettings EXISTS at mcp.server.fastmcp.server.AuthSettings (Verified)
TokenVerifier EXISTS at mcp.server.auth.provider.TokenVerifier (Verified)
AccessToken EXISTS at mcp.server.auth.provider.AccessToken (Verified)
Specific field names (issuer_url, resource_server_url, required_scopes, resource, token_verifier kwarg) are UNVERIFIED — resolve_symbol cannot probe instance attributes
Plan states 'Model the framework on the SDK's own TokenVerifier/AuthSettings seam (the blessed extension point the simple-auth example uses)' — citing an example but not quoting field names
mcp pin is mcp>=1.9 (pyproject.toml line 82); field-level API may differ across versions
findings.finding: The plan calls `FastMCP(token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…), auth=AuthSettings(issuer_url=…, resource_server_url=…, required_scopes=…))` and relies on `AccessToken.resource` as the audience field, but the specific constructor kwarg names (`token_verifier`, `issuer_url`, `resource_server_url`, `required_scopes`) and the `AccessToken.resource` field name are UNVERIFIED at the field level — only the class-level existence of `AuthSettings`, `TokenVerifier`, and `AccessToken` is confirmed. The plan cites the 'simple-auth example' as grounding but does not quote the actual field names from the SDK source. If any field name differs (e.g. `audience` instead of `resource`, or the FastMCP constructor does not accept `token_verifier` directly), the wiring and the composite audience re-check both silently fail or raise at startup.
findings.location: What section — 'Wire it in `build_server`' paragraph; Security section — 'the composite audience re-check (every accepted token's `resource` == `auth_resource_server_url`)'
scenarios: FastMCP constructor does not accept `token_verifier` as a kwarg in mcp>=1.9 — auth middleware is never wired, every request is unauthenticated, no error at startup
AuthSettings does not have an `issuer_url` or `resource_server_url` field — startup raises TypeError or silently uses defaults
AccessToken has no `.resource` field — the composite re-check always raises AttributeError, treating every token as non-accepted (fail-closed, but mis-behaved)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a grounding note in the Approach section: 'Verified against installed mcp X.Y.Z: FastMCP.__init__ accepts token_verifier=<TokenVerifier> and auth=<AuthSettings>; AuthSettings fields: issuer_url, resource_server_url, required_scopes; AccessToken fields include: resource.' This removes the gap for all downstream passes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify against the installed mcp SDK source (or the SDK's own test suite / changelog) that (a) `FastMCP` wired with `auth=AuthSettings(…)` auto-serves `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…`; (b) the response JSON contains `authorization_servers`; and (c) 401 responses include a `WWW-Authenticate` header with a `resource_metadata` URL — quote the confirmed path and field names in AC2/AC3.
evidence: mcp.server.auth.middleware EXISTS (Verified)
The specific URL path /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/ and the JSON shape with authorization_servers are UNVERIFIED against --help or SDK docs
Plan says 'the SDK serves the RFC 9728 PRM (a SINGLE authorization_servers entry derived from issuer_url)' — asserted as SDK behavior, not plan-implemented behavior
EVIDENCE CLASS: Partially-verified
findings.finding: The integration test in AC2/AC3 asserts the SDK automatically serves the RFC 9728 Protected Resource Metadata document at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…` and emits a `WWW-Authenticate` header carrying the `resource_metadata` URL when auth is enabled — both are claimed as SDK-provided behaviors triggered purely by passing `auth=AuthSettings(…)` to FastMCP. Whether mcp>=1.9 actually auto-serves this endpoint and header at that URL path when wired this way is PARTIALLY-VERIFIED: `mcp.server.auth.middleware` resolves, but the specific URL path, the `authorization_servers` JSON field, and the auto-wiring mechanism are unconfirmed against any SDK documentation or source inspection.
findings.location: AC2: 'Integration test asserts the PRM is served at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…`'; AC1: 'unauthenticated → 401 + a `WWW-Authenticate` header carrying the RFC 9728 `resource_metadata` URL'
scenarios: mcp>=1.9 serves the PRM at a different path (e.g. /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource without the trailing resource-server segment) — AC3 oracle fails
The SDK emits WWW-Authenticate but without the resource_metadata URL scheme expected by AC1 — AC1 oracle value check fails
The PRM JSON uses a different field name than authorization_servers — AC3 oracle fails

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the token-generation helper is in S2 scope or S7 scope: if S2, add an AC (e.g., '`rebar gen-token` outputs a ≥128-bit hex token to stdout and exits 0'); if S7, remove the deliverable language from the S2 body and replace with a cross-reference.
evidence: 'provide a `rebar` helper/documented one-liner to generate one (S7 documents the entropy requirement and that static tokens are non-expiring, so `token_env` is preferred)'
No AC references a token-generation helper, CLI subcommand, or one-liner.
Scope section says 'Subset of the epic. Depends on S1.' but does not explicitly exclude the helper from S2.
findings.finding: The plan body says 'provide a `rebar` helper/documented one-liner to generate [a static token]' but this deliverable has no corresponding acceptance criterion and no explicit scope decision — it is unclear whether S2 must ship a working `rebar` CLI subcommand/helper or only a documentation snippet, leaving the executor to choose.
findings.location: What section — Static-token secrets format paragraph, last sentence; Scope section ('S7 documents the entropy requirement')
scenarios: Executor implements a full `rebar gen-token` CLI command as part of S2, causing scope creep.
Executor omits it entirely assuming it belongs to S7, but the S7 plan expects S2 to have provided it — creating a gap between stories.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'provide a `rebar` helper/documented one-liner to generate one (S7 documents…)' with either: (a) 'S7 will provide a `rebar gen-token` helper and document the entropy requirement; S2 only validates the ≥128-bit constraint at startup.' — and add no AC; or (b) add an AC: '`rebar gen-token` (or equivalent one-liner in CONTRIBUTING) produces a ≥256-bit hex token; a smoke test captures stdout and asserts length ≥ 64 hex chars.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the `rebar` token-generation helper is in S2 or S7: if S2, add an AC (e.g., 'a `rebar` CLI subcommand or documented one-liner generates a ≥128-bit token and prints it to stdout'); if S7, remove 'provide a `rebar` helper' from S2's 'What' section and replace with 'document the one-liner for use with the static verifier'.
evidence: 'What': 'provide a `rebar` helper/documented one-liner to generate one (S7 documents the entropy requirement and that static tokens are non-expiring, so `token_env` is preferred)'
ABSENCE: no AC in the AC list references a token-generation helper, CLI command, or one-liner
Scope section: 'The auth framework … and the static verifier only' — does not mention a generation helper
S7 is referenced parenthetically but only for documentation, while 'provide' implies implementation
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes a deliverable — 'provide a `rebar` helper/documented one-liner to generate [a static token]' — but no AC covers implementing, testing, or validating the existence of this helper. It is unclear whether it is in scope for S2 (making it an uncovered deliverable) or deferred to S7 (making the 'What' reference misleading).
findings.location: 'What' section, StaticBearerVerifier paragraph ('provide a `rebar` helper/documented one-liner to generate one (S7 documents the entropy requirement…)')
scenarios: An implementer reads 'provide a rebar helper' and builds it in S2, but S7 also describes it, creating duplicate work
An implementer reads the S7 parenthetical and defers it, leaving the 'What' description partially unimplemented with no AC to catch the gap

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a `CLAUDE.md` / design-doc section) capturing the composite-verifier architecture decision: why the composite is the sole audience+fail-closed choke point, the SDK `.resource` non-inspection constraint grounded at mcp 1.28.1, and what must hold if the SDK version changes.
evidence: 'the SDK's BearerAuthBackend never inspects `.resource` (grounded against mcp 1.28.1) — there is no backstop behind the verifier'

ABSENCE: the plan mentions regenerating `docs/mcp-reference.md`/`docs/env-vars.md` and references S7 for entropy/usage docs, but contains no mention of creating or updating an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc for the composite-verifier pattern itself
The Security section calls this 'Boundary-crossing' with multiple enforcement invariants — a persistent record is warranted
findings.finding: The composite-verifier architecture introduces a security-critical, novel architectural decision — that the SDK's `BearerAuthBackend` never inspects `.resource` (grounded against mcp 1.28.1) and therefore the composite is the ONLY audience + fail-closed backstop — but no ADR or CLAUDE.md update is planned to capture this decision persistently. If this SDK behavior changes or a future contributor bypasses the composite, there is no durable record of why the composite is mandatory.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section; Security section; no ADR/CLAUDE.md update mentioned anywhere in the plan
scenarios: A future contributor adds a new verifier (S3–S6) and reasons they can skip the composite, not knowing the SDK doesn't backstop `.resource`
SDK upgrades to mcp 1.29+ potentially start inspecting `.resource` — without an ADR, the composite's rationale is invisible and the duplicate check may be silently dropped
Onboarding a security reviewer who needs to understand the trust model finds only inline prose in a story ticket, not a durable doc
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-composite-token-verifier.md` (or equivalent) documenting: (1) the SDK gap (`BearerAuthBackend` does not check `token.resource`), (2) the composite as the only enforcement point, (3) the invariant that every new verifier (S3–S6) MUST be wrapped by the composite and never called directly, (4) the SDK version constraint and how to re-evaluate if upgraded past 1.28.x.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one observable instrumentation hook (e.g., a logged structured event with a stable `event` key, or an incrementable counter) for: auth failure (401 issued), audience mismatch, and verifier exception — so operators can alert on or query auth health without full log-scraping.
evidence: Plan: 'add a redaction helper used on every auth error/log path' — only logging is mentioned.
No metrics, counters, tracing spans, or alerting hooks are specified for the auth path.
Observability criterion requires 'new failure points instrumented with a metric/log/trace/alert'.
findings.finding: The plan specifies logging for auth errors (composite raise-handler, verifier errors) but mentions no structured metrics or observable signals (e.g., a counter for auth failures, verifier errors, or audience mismatches) that would let operators detect auth misconfiguration or an attack in production.
findings.location: Security section; 'No-log redaction' bullet in What section
scenarios: A buggy custom verifier starts raising on every request; composite silently demotes all to 401 with no metric signal. An operator has no way to detect the misconfiguration without log-scraping.
An attacker probes the static token endpoint; no counter makes rate-limit detection or alerting impossible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the spec (and in S7 docs) that the digest map is immutable after startup and that token rotation (add/revoke) requires a server restart; document the expected rotation procedure and any revocation latency.
evidence: Plan says the verifier 'reads the secrets file… stores only SHA-256 digests' at load time, with no mention of runtime reload or restart requirement for token rotation.
S7 documentation scope is mentioned for entropy requirements but not for rotation lifecycle.
T9 checklist: 'Consumers enumerated and tolerant of absence/staleness' and 'RETIRE/cleanup path' are not addressed for the digest map.
findings.finding: The lifecycle of the StaticBearerVerifier's digest map — specifically that token rotation requires a full server restart (no hot-reload) — is not stated. This is a T9 UPDATE/RETIRE gap: consumers (operators) are not told the rotation model, and if hot-reload were ever assumed, the current immutable-at-init design would be incorrect.
findings.location: What section — StaticBearerVerifier; Compatibility section
scenarios: An operator adds a new static token to the secrets file expecting it to take effect without restart; token is silently ignored until restart.
A revoked token remains valid until restart with no stated TTL or reload mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the StaticBearerVerifier spec: 'The digest map is built once at startup and is immutable; adding or revoking a static token requires a server restart. Operators should treat restart as the rotation primitive and document this in runbooks.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC line (or explicit scope note deferring to S7) for the `rebar` static-token generation helper: specify its invocation, expected output format (e.g., prints a `token_env`-ready hex string), and what happens on error (bad args / no entropy source), so the deliverable is observable and its UX non-happy-path is defined.
evidence: 'provide a `rebar` helper/documented one-liner to generate one (S7 documents the entropy requirement and that static tokens are non-expiring, so `token_env` is preferred)'

No AC line references the helper's existence, invocation, output format, or error behavior — S7 is cited only for documentation of the entropy requirement, leaving the helper's scope boundary ambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan mentions 'provide a `rebar` helper/documented one-liner to generate [a static token]' as an S2 deliverable, but no AC line verifies that this helper exists, produces correct output, or handles error inputs (e.g., bad entropy source, wrong invocation). This is a non-deferred goal without an end-state-observable proof.
findings.location: Static-token secrets format section ('provide a rebar helper/documented one-liner to generate one') and Acceptance Criteria (no corresponding AC line)
scenarios: An implementer ships S2 without the helper (treating it as S7 scope) and there is no test to catch the omission.

An implementer ships the helper but with no specified behavior for invalid invocation (e.g., calling it with no arguments, or on a platform where /dev/urandom is unavailable) — the UX of the primary token-creation flow is unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: '`rebar gen-token` (or equivalent documented one-liner) prints a ≥128-bit hex token to stdout; invoking it with unsupported args exits non-zero with a usage message; the output is suitable for direct use as an env-var value in a `token_env` record.' If the helper is truly S7, add an explicit sentence: 'The generation helper is deferred to S7; S2 only documents the manual openssl/python one-liner.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the exact import paths and field signatures for `TokenVerifier`, `AuthSettings`, and `AccessToken` from the installed mcp SDK (e.g., by reading the installed site-packages source), and specify the minimum mcp version that includes auth support in the plan.

resolve_symbol('TokenVerifier', 'mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('AuthSettings', 'mcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('AuthSettings', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('AuthSettings', 'mcp.server.fastmcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('AccessToken', 'mcp') → UNRESOLVED
resolve_symbol('AccessToken', 'mcp.server.auth') → UNRESOLVED
search_files for 'simple-auth' → no matches in repo

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Model the framework on the SDK's own TokenVerifier/AuthSettings seam (the blessed extension point the simple-auth example uses)' — TokenVerifier and AuthSettings are not resolvable from any mcp submodule in the installed environment, and no 'simple-auth' reference exists anywhere in the repo. These symbols are the foundational extension points the entire auth framework's design rests on.
findings.location: Approach & rationale: 'Model the framework on the SDK's own TokenVerifier/AuthSettings seam (the blessed extension point the simple-auth example uses)'
scenarios: TokenVerifier/AuthSettings exist in the SDK but under a different module path than assumed — the import statements in _mcp_auth.py will fail at load time.
The SDK's auth extension seam was added in a later minor version than mcp>=1.9 — deployments on the minimum supported version would fail to start with auth enabled.
The AccessToken dataclass fields (resource, client_id, scopes, expires_at) assumed throughout the plan may differ from the actual SDK model.
findings.suggested_fix: Locate the installed mcp package (e.g., `python -c 'import mcp; print(mcp.__file__)'`) and grep its `auth` submodule for the actual class names and module paths, then anchor the plan's import statements to those verified paths.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
